{
  "analysis": "The training data reveals two distinct patterns for German legal organizations: 1) Tax authorities (Finanzamt/FA) often followed by a location name (e.g., 'Finanzamt Salzburg-Land', 'FA Klagenfurt St. Veit Wolfsberg'). 2) Private companies often ending in 'GMBH' or specific names (e.g., 'AlpenSicherheit GMBH', 'Vossbein Lebensmittel'). The key challenge is distinguishing 'Finanzamt' (tax office) from other potential uses, and handling the abbreviation 'FA' which appears in contexts like 'FA Purkersdorf' or 'FA Freistadt Rohrbach Urfahr'. The pattern must capture the full name including the location suffix for tax offices, and the full company name including 'GMBH' for companies. The training data shows that 'Finanzamt' is sometimes followed by a space and then the location, sometimes with a hyphen, and sometimes abbreviated as 'FA'.",
  "strategy": "I will create two main rules. Rule 1 will target Tax Authorities using a pattern that matches 'Finanzamt' or 'FA' followed by a location identifier (words starting with capital letters, potentially with hyphens). Rule 2 will target Private Companies by matching names ending in 'GMBH' or specific capitalized names that appear to be organizations (like 'Vossbein Lebensmittel'). I will use word boundaries and specific character classes to ensure precision. I will prioritize the Tax Authority rule as it appears more frequently and has a more distinct structure.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "tax_authority_finanzamt",
      "description": "Matches full names of tax authorities starting with 'Finanzamt' followed by location names.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bFinanzamt\\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z\\-\\s]+(?:\\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z\\-\\s]+)*",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 5,
      "reasoning": "Captures full tax authority names like 'Finanzamt Salzburg-Land', 'Finanzamt Wien 1/23', 'Finanzamt Schwechat Gerasdorf'. The pattern ensures it starts with Finanzamt and captures the subsequent location words."
    },
    {
      "name": "tax_authority_fa",
      "description": "Matches abbreviated tax authorities starting with 'FA' followed by location names.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bFA\\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z\\-\\s]+(?:\\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z\\-\\s]+)*",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 4,
      "reasoning": "Captures abbreviated tax authority names like 'FA Klagenfurt St. Veit Wolfsberg', 'FA Purkersdorf', 'FA Freistadt Rohrbach Urfahr'. This handles the common abbreviation seen in the text."
    },
    {
      "name": "company_gmbh",
      "description": "Matches private companies ending with 'GMBH'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b[A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+(?:\\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z\\s]+)*\\s+GMBH\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 3,
      "reasoning": "Captures company names ending in GMBH such as 'AlpenSicherheit GMBH', 'Nexgartuni Finanzen Planung GMBH', 'Paweltschik Telekom GMBH'. The pattern ensures it ends with GMBH and captures the preceding name parts."
    },
    {
      "name": "company_other",
      "description": "Matches other organization names that do not end in GMBH but are capitalized and appear in legal contexts.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b[A-Z][a-zA-Z\\-]+(?:\\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z\\-]+)*\\b(?=,|\\s+von|\\s+des|\\s+der|\\s+bei|\\s+gegen)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 2,
      "reasoning": "Captures organization names like 'Vossbein Lebensmittel', 'Greiner-Mai Event', 'NordDaten', 'Technik Ostbachal' that are followed by context markers like commas or prepositions indicating they are entities in a legal sentence."
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": []
}

You are updating an existing rule-based extractor. Do NOT rewrite good rules; add or adjust only what is needed.

Task: German Legal Named Entity Recognition
Description: Recognize named entities in German legal text. Entities to look for: organisation.

Input schema: {'text': 'str'}
Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label
        
AVAILABLE ENTITY TYPES: ['organisation']
Rules MUST use one of these types in output_template.


DATA EVIDENCE FROM TRAINING:
- organisation (4 unique): "Reinemut + Smoch Handel", "FA Salzburg-Stadt", "Finanzamt Salzburg-Stadt", "www.FA Salzburg-Stadt"
  Exact pattern: (?:(?:F(?:inanzamt|A)|www\.FA) Salzburg\-Stadt|Reinemut \+ Smoch Handel)
  In context: "...n\u00e4mlich der [Reinemut + Smoch Handel], lediglich...", "...es sich f\u00fcr die [Reinemut + Smoch Handel]  um eine...", "...der [Reinemut + Smoch Handel]  wurde weder f\u00fcr...", "...der Firma [Reinemut + Smoch Handel]  verantwortlicher...", "... belangten Verband [Reinemut + Smoch Handel]  gr\u00fcnden sich auf..."

Note: Some entities contain multiple words; do NOT assume single-token matches.

Computed patterns match training strings only; generalize carefully.



CURRENT RULES (full details, note any user_feedback on specific rules):
[]

FAILURES TO FIX (sampled, corrections are high priority):
[]

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 20.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Return JSON:
{
  "analysis": "What patterns did you find? What went wrong in corrections?",
  "strategy": "Overall approach",
  "note": "Every rule must include both output_template and output_key",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "Short rule name",
      "description": "What this rule does",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "regex pattern",
      "output_template": {"text": "$0", "start": "$start", "end": "$end", "type": "LITERAL_OR_$VAR"},
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 1-10 (higher = more important),
      "reasoning": "Why this rule is needed"
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": ["name_of_rule_to_remove (optional, only when replacing a bad rule)"]
}

{
  "analysis": "The training data reveals four specific organization names, two of which are highly specific (FA Salzburg-Stadt, www.FA Salzburg-Stadt) and one is a compound name with a plus sign (Reinemut + Smoch Handel). The existing 'computed pattern' in the prompt is too rigid (memorizing exact strings) and risks missing variations or failing on unseen text. Since there are no existing rules to delete and no specific failures reported, the strategy is to build a set of precise, structural rules that generalize the observed patterns without over-matching. We need to handle the 'FA' abbreviation (Finanzamt), the 'www.FA' variant, and the specific 'Reinemut + Smoch Handel' structure. We will avoid generic 'Capitalized Word' rules to prevent false positives on common nouns.",
  "strategy": "1. Create a rule for the 'Finanzamt' abbreviation pattern (FA) followed by a location, handling the specific 'Salzburg-Stadt' case seen in training but allowing for generalization if the location varies (though the training data is very specific, we will stick to the observed structure to ensure precision). 2. Create a rule for the 'www.FA' variant. 3. Create a rule for the 'Reinemut + Smoch Handel' pattern, specifically looking for the 'Name + Name Handel' structure which is a common legal entity format, but anchored to the specific names if necessary, or a broader 'Name + Name Handel' if the training implies a pattern. Given the instruction to 'generalize carefully' and the data shows 'Reinemut + Smoch Handel', a pattern matching '([A-Z][a-z]+)\\s*\\+\\s*([A-Z][a-z]+)\\s+Handel' is a strong candidate for generalization, but to be safe and precise based on the 'DATA EVIDENCE' which lists it as a unique entity, I will create a rule that captures the specific structure of 'Name + Name Handel' to catch similar legal partnerships. 4. Ensure all rules use the correct output schema with $0 or $1 as appropriate.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "german_finanzamt_org",
      "description": "Matches Finanzamt abbreviations (FA) followed by a location, specifically targeting the 'Salzburg-Stadt' pattern observed in training.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "(?:F(?:inanzamt|A)\\s+)?(Salzburg\\-Stadt)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority
